Question: What is the Response Time Analysis project?
Answer: GitHub repository: https://github.com/iNoahCodeGuy/response_time_cl_analysis. The question every sales team argues about: does responding faster to leads actually increase the chance of closing a deal? Most teams look at the numbers and see that fast responses have higher close rates. Case closed, right? Not even close. The problem is confounding. Walk-in leads are high intent and get responded to quickly. Top-performing reps are both faster and better closers. A naive comparison of fast versus slow responses conflates speed with lead quality and rep skill. This app is a five-layer statistical argument that systematically separates the real effect of speed from everything else that looks like speed. It is a Streamlit application that accepts any sales team's lead data, runs five layers of increasingly rigorous analysis, and tells you not just whether the pattern exists but whether it survives after you control for the variables that could fake it. Every statistical test includes the formula, the result, and a plain-English explanation. The target user is a sales manager who needs the answer, not a statistician who already knows how to get it. ![Executive dashboard showing close rates, volume, distribution, and weekly trends](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/iNoahCodeGuy/response_time_cl_analysis/main/figures/10_executive_dashboard.png)

Question: What is confounding and why does it matter in the Response Time Analysis project?
Answer: Confounding is the single most important concept in this project. Here is the problem in plain terms: imagine you notice that people who carry umbrellas get rained on more often. Does carrying an umbrella cause rain? Obviously not. Rain causes both the umbrella-carrying and the getting-rained-on. The umbrella and the rain are confounded by a third variable: the weather forecast. The same logic applies to lead response times. Fast responses correlate with higher close rates. But walk-in leads are high intent (they already showed up) AND they get responded to quickly (the person is standing in front of you). The lead source confounds the relationship between speed and closing. Similarly, the best sales reps tend to be both faster at responding and better at closing. Rep skill confounds the relationship. A naive analysis says 'responding in under 15 minutes triples your close rate.' The honest analysis says 'responding in under 15 minutes increases your close rate by 2.3x after controlling for lead source, and the effect is real, but it is smaller than the raw numbers suggest.' The difference between 3x and 2.3x is confounding. The enterprise dataset demonstrates this precisely: the fastest bucket's odds ratio drops from 3.17x (uncontrolled) to 2.30x (controlled for lead source). The effect shrinks but survives. That is the finding: speed matters independently, but not as much as the raw numbers claim. ![Model comparison showing uncontrolled vs controlled odds ratios](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/iNoahCodeGuy/response_time_cl_analysis/main/figures/09_model_comparison.png)

Question: How does the five-layer statistical system work?
Answer: Five layers, each answering a more precise question than the last. The progression matters because each layer addresses a specific weakness in the one before it. Layer 1 is descriptive statistics: group leads into response time buckets (0-15, 15-30, 30-60, 60+ minutes), calculate close rates per bucket with Wilson score confidence intervals, and look at the pattern. In the enterprise dataset: 14.9% close rate for 0-15 minutes, 10.2% for 15-30, 7.5% for 30-60, 5.2% for 60+. A clear gradient. But this layer cannot tell you whether the differences are statistically significant or just noise. ![Close rates by response time bucket with confidence intervals](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/iNoahCodeGuy/response_time_cl_analysis/main/figures/01_close_rate_by_bucket.png) Layer 2 is hypothesis testing: chi-square test asks whether response bucket and close outcome are independent. If the p-value is above 0.05, the pattern could be random and you stop. If it is below 0.05, you know something is there. Pairwise z-tests with Bonferroni correction then identify which specific buckets differ from each other. But this layer cannot tell you whether the effect is caused by speed or by something else correlated with speed. ![Pairwise z-test matrix showing all comparisons significant](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/iNoahCodeGuy/response_time_cl_analysis/main/figures/08_pairwise_ztest_matrix.png) Layer 3 is confounding control: logistic regression models the probability of closing as a function of response bucket AND lead source simultaneously. This isolates the speed effect from the lead quality effect. The enterprise dataset shows the fastest bucket's odds ratio drops from 3.17x to 2.30x after controlling for lead source, confirming confounding exists but the speed effect persists independently. ![Forest plot of odds ratios with 95% confidence intervals](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/iNoahCodeGuy/response_time_cl_analysis/main/figures/05_forest_plot_odds_ratios.png) Layer 4 assesses the confounding structure directly: a heatmap of close rates by source and bucket shows whether the speed gradient exists within every lead source independently. If it does, the effect is not just a between-source artifact. The rep scatter plot checks whether faster reps are simply better closers. ![Heatmap of close rates by lead source and response bucket](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/iNoahCodeGuy/response_time_cl_analysis/main/figures/04_heatmap_source_x_bucket.png) Layer 5 is temporal consistency: weekly trends track close rates by bucket over 24 weeks. If the effect is real, it should be stable. If it is driven by one anomalous week, the entire argument weakens. ![Weekly trend showing fastest bucket consistently on top across 24 weeks](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/iNoahCodeGuy/response_time_cl_analysis/main/figures/07_weekly_trend.png)

Question: What tools and technologies were used in the Response Time Analysis project?
Answer: Python 3, Streamlit (web framework and interactive UI), Pandas (data manipulation), NumPy (numerical operations), Plotly (interactive charts with hover, zoom, and drill-down), SciPy (chi-square tests, normal distribution functions), Statsmodels (logistic regression and proportion z-tests with Bonferroni correction), python-dateutil (flexible date parsing including ISO, US, European, and Excel serial number formats), openpyxl (Excel file support). The modular architecture spans 26 Python files across five directories: data/ for loading, parsing, and column auto-detection, analysis/ for all statistical computations, explanations/ for first-principles interpretation of every test, components/ for UI elements, and config/ for all tunable parameters. Static publication-quality figures are generated separately via generate_figures.py using Plotly's write_image at 2x resolution.

Question: What is the chi-square test and how is it used in the Response Time Analysis?
Answer: The chi-square test of independence is the screening test, the first statistical question: is there any relationship between how fast you respond and whether the lead closes? Think of it like this. You have a table with response time buckets as rows and outcomes (closed or not closed) as columns. If speed does not matter, you would expect the close rate to be roughly the same across all buckets. The chi-square test compares what you actually observed against what you would expect if speed had zero effect. The bigger the gap between observed and expected, the larger the chi-square statistic, and the smaller the p-value. If the p-value is below 0.05, the pattern is unlikely to be random chance. In the enterprise dataset with 75,000 leads, the chi-square test is highly significant across all comparisons. The app shows the full worked example: observed frequencies, expected frequencies under independence, per-bucket contributions to the chi-square statistic, and the critical value comparison. The important limitation: chi-square tells you the relationship exists but not how strong it is, which bucket drives it, or whether it survives after controlling for other variables. That is why it is Layer 2 in a five-layer system, not the whole analysis.

Question: What is the pairwise z-test and Bonferroni correction?
Answer: The chi-square test says 'something differs somewhere.' The pairwise z-test says 'here is exactly where.' It compares every pair of response time buckets directly: is the close rate for 0-15 minutes significantly different from 15-30 minutes? From 30-60? From 60+? With four buckets, that is six separate comparisons. The statistical problem: every time you run a test, there is a 5% chance of a false positive. Run six tests, and the chance of at least one false positive rises to nearly 26%. Bonferroni correction solves this by dividing the significance threshold by the number of comparisons (0.05 / 6 = 0.0083). A result has to clear a higher bar to be called significant. It is the conservative approach. If something passes Bonferroni, you can trust it. In the enterprise dataset, all six pairwise comparisons are significant at p < 0.001, well below even the Bonferroni-corrected threshold. The differences range from 2.3 percentage points (between adjacent buckets) to 9.7 percentage points (fastest versus slowest). The app also calculates Cohen's h for each comparison, a standardized effect size that tells you whether the difference is small, medium, or large regardless of sample size. ![Pairwise z-test matrix with significance levels and effect sizes](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/iNoahCodeGuy/response_time_cl_analysis/main/figures/08_pairwise_ztest_matrix.png)

Question: What is logistic regression and how does it control for confounding?
Answer: Logistic regression is the critical test in the system. It answers the question the earlier tests cannot: does speed matter after you account for other factors? Here is the intuition. Imagine you are trying to figure out whether coffee causes heart attacks. People who drink more coffee also tend to smoke more and exercise less. If you just compare coffee drinkers to non-drinkers, you are also comparing smokers to non-smokers and sedentary people to active people. Logistic regression lets you hold smoking and exercise constant and ask: among people with the same smoking and exercise habits, does coffee still matter? In this project, the model predicts close probability from response time bucket AND lead source simultaneously. The formula is: probability of closing = function of (response bucket + lead source). The reference category is the slowest bucket (60+ minutes), so every other bucket's odds ratio tells you how much better the odds of closing are compared to the slowest responders. The enterprise dataset result: the fastest bucket (0-15 minutes) has a 2.30x odds ratio after controlling for lead source. That means leads responded to within 15 minutes have 2.3 times the odds of closing compared to 60+ minute responses, holding lead source constant. Without the control, the raw ratio was 3.17x. The 0.87x difference is confounding, real but quantified. The model also reports 95% confidence intervals, z-statistics, and p-values for every coefficient. Pseudo R-squared of 0.073 means the model explains a modest portion of variance, which is expected because many factors beyond speed and source influence whether a deal closes. ![Forest plot showing odds ratios with confidence intervals on log scale](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/iNoahCodeGuy/response_time_cl_analysis/main/figures/05_forest_plot_odds_ratios.png)

Question: What does the heatmap reveal about lead source and response time?
Answer: The heatmap is the confounding diagnostic. It answers a question the logistic regression raises but does not directly show: does the speed gradient exist within every lead source, or only between them? If walk-in leads close at 20% regardless of speed while website leads close at 5% regardless of speed, then the overall pattern is entirely driven by lead mix, not response time. The heatmap would show flat rows and different row heights. But in the enterprise dataset, the heatmap shows a green-to-red gradient within every single lead source. Walk-ins: 27.6% close rate at 0-15 minutes versus 13.4% at 60+ minutes. Website forms: 12.1% versus 4.2%. Social media ads: 9.6% versus 3.4%. The gradient is steeper for some sources than others, but it exists everywhere. This is the visual proof that the speed effect is not just a lead-mix artifact. Even when you hold lead source constant by looking at one row at a time, faster responses still close more. The heatmap answers a question that even the logistic regression coefficient cannot show as intuitively: the pattern is pervasive, not driven by one dominant lead source. ![Heatmap showing close rate gradient within every lead source](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/iNoahCodeGuy/response_time_cl_analysis/main/figures/04_heatmap_source_x_bucket.png)

Question: What does the sales rep scatter plot show?
Answer: The rep scatter plot is the other confounding diagnostic, this time asking about people instead of lead sources. Each dot is a sales rep. The x-axis is their median response time, the y-axis is their close rate, and the bubble size shows how many leads they handled. A trend line and correlation coefficient summarize the relationship. In the enterprise dataset with 35 reps, the correlation is r = -0.93. That is nearly perfect: faster reps close more, almost without exception. This is both the strongest finding and the biggest confounding red flag. If the best closers are also the fastest responders, you cannot tell from this data alone whether speed causes closes or whether skilled reps happen to do both. The logistic regression helps disentangle this by controlling for lead source, but rep skill is harder to isolate because it affects everything simultaneously. The scatter plot makes this visible at a glance. A manager looking at this chart sees two things: which reps are outliers (fast but low close rate, or slow but high close rate), and whether the overall pattern is tight enough to take seriously. At r = -0.93, the pattern is not subtle. ![Sales rep scatter plot showing r = -0.93 correlation between speed and close rate](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/iNoahCodeGuy/response_time_cl_analysis/main/figures/06_rep_scatter.png)

Question: What are Wilson score confidence intervals and why use them?
Answer: Confidence intervals tell you how much uncertainty is in a number. If the 0-15 minute bucket has a 14.9% close rate, the confidence interval might be 14.2% to 15.6%. The interval says 'the true rate is probably somewhere in this range.' If two buckets' intervals overlap, the difference might not be real. Wilson score intervals are specifically designed for proportions (percentages). The naive approach is to use the normal approximation: take the proportion plus or minus a multiple of the standard error. That works fine when the proportion is near 50% and the sample is large. But close rates are often low (5-15%), and some buckets may have small samples. The normal approximation can produce impossible results like negative lower bounds. The Wilson score interval solves this by using a formula that stays between 0% and 100% and is more accurate when proportions are far from 50% or samples are modest. It is the standard in modern statistical software. In the close rate bar chart, the error bars are Wilson score intervals. When they do not overlap between buckets, you can be confident the difference is statistically meaningful even before running formal tests.

Question: What data does the Response Time Analysis app accept?
Answer: The app accepts CSV, XLSX, and XLS files. Required fields: lead arrival time, first response time, lead source, sales rep, and order outcome. The column mapper uses fuzzy matching against common naming patterns, so a column called 'created_at' automatically maps to lead time, 'rep_name' maps to sales rep, and so on. Date formats are auto-detected including ISO (2024-01-15), US (01/15/2024), European (15/01/2024), and even Excel serial numbers. Outcomes can be boolean, 1/0, or Yes/No. Three bucket presets are available: Standard (0/15/30/60+ minutes), Aggressive (0/5/15/30+), and Relaxed (0/30/60/120+), or users can define custom boundaries. Outliers are capped at the 99th percentile by default to prevent extreme response times from distorting the analysis. The built-in enterprise sample generator creates 75,000 realistic leads spanning 24 weeks with 8 lead sources, 35 sales reps, and deliberately embedded confounding, so the app demonstrates the full analytical power without requiring proprietary data. A simpler generator creates 10,000 leads with randomized effect strength between 0.0 and 1.5, meaning some generated datasets show significant effects and some do not. The app handles both cases gracefully.

Question: What is the architecture of the Response Time Analysis project?
Answer: 26 Python files organized across five directories, each with a single responsibility. app.py is the Streamlit entry point running a five-step wizard: Upload, Map Columns, Settings, Analyze, Results. config/settings.py holds every configurable parameter: bucket boundaries, alpha levels, color schemes, sample data configuration. data/ contains six modules: loader.py (CSV and Excel ingestion), datetime_parser.py (auto-detects four date formats including Excel serial numbers), column_mapper.py (fuzzy name matching to auto-detect column roles), sample_generator.py (creates realistic data with embedded confounding), weeks_analyzer.py (validates data timespan), and export.py (verification CSV with Excel formulas for independent reproduction). analysis/ contains five modules: preprocessing.py, descriptive.py (Wilson score CIs, cross-tabulations), statistical_tests.py (chi-square, pairwise z-tests with Bonferroni), regression.py (logistic regression with model comparison), and weekly_trends.py (temporal consistency). explanations/ is the most distinctive directory: explainers.py, common.py, templates.py, formulas.py, verification_panels.py, p_value.py, odds_ratio.py, and confidence_intervals.py. Every statistical concept is explained from first principles, not just the result but what the test does, why it was chosen, the math, and the plain-English interpretation. components/ contains the UI layer: results_dashboard.py, charts.py, upload.py, mapping_ui.py, step_display.py, and settings_panel.py.

Question: What is the explanation system in the Response Time Analysis project?
Answer: The explanation system is the most interesting design decision in the project. Most analytics tools show you a p-value and expect you to know what it means. This app assumes you do not. The explanations/ directory contains eight modules dedicated to translating statistical output into plain English. Every test includes three layers of explanation. First, what the test does and why it was chosen for this specific question. Second, the mathematical formula with the actual numbers plugged in. Third, a plain-English interpretation of the result: what does a p-value of 0.003 actually mean for your sales team? The p_value.py module explains significance thresholds. The odds_ratio.py module explains what it means when the odds ratio is 2.3 (your leads responded to within 15 minutes have 2.3 times the odds of closing compared to the slowest group). The confidence_intervals.py module explains why a range matters more than a point estimate. The verification_panels.py module generates downloadable CSV files with embedded Excel formulas so users can independently reproduce every calculation. The target user is a sales director who needs to make a staffing decision based on this data. They need to trust the numbers without having taken a statistics course. The explanation system is what makes that possible.

Question: What are the key findings from the enterprise dataset?
Answer: 75,000 leads, 6,807 orders, 9.1% overall close rate, 35 sales reps, 8 lead sources, 24 weeks of data. The headline finding: leads responded to within 15 minutes close at 14.9%. Leads left waiting over 60 minutes close at 5.2%. That is a 2.9x raw difference. The gradient is monotonic, each slower bucket has a lower close rate with no exceptions. ![Close rate bars showing 14.9%, 10.2%, 7.5%, 5.2% across buckets](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/iNoahCodeGuy/response_time_cl_analysis/main/figures/01_close_rate_by_bucket.png) But the raw numbers overstate the effect. After controlling for lead source via logistic regression, the fastest bucket's odds ratio is 2.30x, not 3.17x. The 0.87x gap is confounding: high-intent lead sources (walk-ins, phone calls) both close more often and get faster responses. The speed effect is real but smaller than naive analysis suggests. ![Uncontrolled vs controlled odds ratios side by side](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/iNoahCodeGuy/response_time_cl_analysis/main/figures/09_model_comparison.png) The heatmap confirms the effect is pervasive: the speed gradient exists within every single lead source, not just between them. Walk-ins: 27.6% to 13.4%. Website forms: 12.1% to 4.2%. The rep scatter plot shows r = -0.93 correlation between median response time and close rate across 35 reps. All six pairwise bucket comparisons are significant at p < 0.001 after Bonferroni correction. The pattern is stable across all 24 weeks with no anomalous periods driving the result. The conclusion: responding faster independently increases close probability, but the magnitude is roughly 2.3x, not the 3x that the raw numbers suggest.

Question: How does the sample data generator work?
Answer: The sample data generator is not just a random number factory. It deliberately embeds the confounding structure that the analysis is designed to detect. Eight lead sources (Website Form, Phone Call, Walk-in, Dealer Referral, Third Party Lead, Social Media Ad, Email Campaign, Chat Widget) each have different base close rates AND different response speeds. Walk-ins have high intent and fast responses. Third-party leads have low intent and slow responses. This creates the confounding that makes naive analysis misleading. Thirty-five sales reps have varying skill levels that affect both their response speed and their close rate, creating the second confounding pathway. Response times follow a log-normal distribution (median roughly 28 minutes) modified by source speed, rep skill, region, and time-of-day effects. The enterprise generator creates 75,000 leads with an effect strength of 1.2 (strong), spanning 24 weeks. The standard generator creates 10,000 leads with randomized effect strength between 0.0 and 1.5, meaning some generated datasets genuinely have no speed effect and the app correctly reports non-significance. This is important: the app is not designed to always find an effect. It is designed to correctly identify when one exists and when it does not.

Question: What is the response time distribution?
Answer: Response times in both real sales data and the generated sample follow a log-normal distribution: most leads get a response within the first hour, but a long tail stretches out to several hours or even days. The distribution is right-skewed by definition. You cannot respond in negative time, but there is no upper limit on how long a lead can wait. The histogram shows this shape with vertical lines marking the bucket boundaries at 15, 30, and 60 minutes. ![Response time distribution showing log-normal shape with bucket boundaries](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/iNoahCodeGuy/response_time_cl_analysis/main/figures/03_response_time_distribution.png) This skew is why the analysis uses nonparametric methods for many tests. Methods that assume normality (like standard ANOVA) would give unreliable results because the data is fundamentally asymmetric. The bucket approach transforms the continuous distribution into ordinal categories, which sidesteps the normality problem entirely. The enterprise dataset has 18,000 leads in the 0-15 minute bucket, 14,800 in 15-30, 16,300 in 30-60, and 25,500 in 60+. The largest bucket is the slowest, reflecting the long tail. ![Sample sizes per bucket](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/iNoahCodeGuy/response_time_cl_analysis/main/figures/02_sample_sizes.png)

Question: What are the limitations of the Response Time Analysis project?
Answer: Four limitations stated directly in the app, because honest methodology includes its own critique. First, correlation does not equal causation. The data is observational. An A/B test where you randomly delay responses would prove causation, but no sales team would agree to deliberately slow down. The logistic regression controls for measured confounders, but unmeasured ones (lead urgency, product interest level, time of day) could still bias the result. Second, selection bias. The data only includes leads that received a response. Leads that were never contacted are invisible. If uncontacted leads are systematically different from contacted ones, the analysis only describes the subset you can see. Third, the confounding control is incomplete. The regression controls for lead source, but rep skill, regional effects, seasonal patterns, and product mix could all influence the result. The model's pseudo R-squared of 0.073 means 92.7% of variance is unexplained, which is normal for human behavior data but worth acknowledging. Fourth, external validity. Patterns from one sales team may not generalize to others with different products, price points, or sales cycles. A 15-minute threshold that matters for car sales might be irrelevant for enterprise software with a 6-month sales cycle.

Question: What is the weekly trend analysis?
Answer: The weekly trend analysis is the temporal consistency check, Layer 5 in the five-layer system. The logic: if the speed effect is real, it should be stable over time. If the entire finding is driven by one anomalous week (a holiday, a promotional event, a data glitch), the pattern disappears when you remove that week. The analysis tracks close rates by response time bucket across all 24 weeks of the enterprise dataset. Four lines, one per bucket, plotted over time. If the fastest bucket's line is consistently above the others with no crossovers, the effect is persistent. ![Weekly trend with four lines showing consistent bucket ordering over 24 weeks](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/iNoahCodeGuy/response_time_cl_analysis/main/figures/07_weekly_trend.png) The module calculates week-over-week changes, detects trends via slope analysis, and checks whether the bucket ordering is maintained across weeks. In the enterprise dataset, the fastest bucket is on top for all 24 weeks with no periods of reversal. Week-to-week variation exists (this is real data with noise), but the ordering never flips. A sales manager looking at this chart can be confident the finding is not seasonal or event-driven. It is a persistent structural pattern in how speed relates to outcomes.

Question: How does the Response Time Analysis project compare to the Lead Response Heatmap?
Answer: Same domain, opposite analytical questions, complementary conclusions. The Lead Response Heatmap (GitHub: https://github.com/iNoahCodeGuy/generic-lead-response-heatmap) answers when is my team fast and when is it slow. It is descriptive analytics: a color-coded grid of day-of-week by hour-of-day showing where response time coverage gaps exist. Tuesday afternoons are red. Saturday mornings are green. The Response Time Analysis app answers does the speed difference actually cost you money. It is inferential analytics: five layers of statistical tests culminating in a confounding-controlled estimate of how much speed matters. The heatmap without the statistical proof is just a pattern with no proven consequence. Maybe Tuesday afternoon slowdowns do not affect close rates. The statistical proof without the heatmap gives you a number (2.3x odds ratio) but no operational guidance on where to fix it. Together they form a complete argument. The heatmap identifies the specific hours where your team is slow. The Response Time Analysis proves that slowness costs you a measurable number of deals. The combination turns a statistical finding into a staffing decision. Noah built both because the analytical workflow requires both halves.

Question: What is the design philosophy behind the Response Time Analysis project?
Answer: Complexity proportional to the problem. The Lead Response Heatmap is 500 lines across 3 files because descriptive analytics is structurally simple. The Response Time Analysis is 26 files because inferential statistics requires more machinery: five sequential analytical layers, configurable parameters, mathematical formula rendering, and a full explanation system that translates every statistical concept from first principles. The app is designed around a specific user: a sales director who needs to make a staffing decision. They do not have a statistics background. They need to trust the numbers. That constraint drove two design decisions. First, the explanation system: every test includes what it does, why it was chosen, the math with actual numbers plugged in, and a plain-English interpretation. Second, the verification CSV export: users can download a spreadsheet with embedded Excel formulas that reproduce every calculation independently. Trust through transparency, not through authority. The sample data generator embeds realistic confounding so the app demonstrates the full analytical power without requiring proprietary data. The five-step wizard (Upload, Map, Settings, Analyze, Results) means the app is usable in under two minutes. Column auto-detection, date format auto-detection, and sensible defaults handle the friction that normally kills analytics tools before they deliver value.

Question: What are odds ratios and how are they interpreted?
Answer: An odds ratio is the most intuitive way to express how much one group differs from another in terms of probability. If the fastest bucket has a 2.30x odds ratio compared to the slowest bucket, it means: the odds of closing a deal are 2.3 times higher when you respond within 15 minutes versus waiting over 60 minutes. Odds are not the same as probability. If the close rate is 14.9%, the odds are 14.9 / 85.1 = 0.175, or roughly 1 in 5.7. For the slowest bucket at 5.2%, the odds are 5.2 / 94.8 = 0.055, or roughly 1 in 18.2. The odds ratio is 0.175 / 0.055 = 3.17 (the raw, uncontrolled ratio). After logistic regression controls for lead source, the ratio drops to 2.30. The forest plot shows odds ratios on a log scale with 95% confidence intervals. A vertical line at OR = 1.0 marks no effect. Points to the right of that line mean faster responses increase close odds. If the confidence interval crosses the 1.0 line, the effect is not statistically significant. In the enterprise dataset, all three faster buckets have confidence intervals entirely to the right of 1.0, confirming the speed effect at every level: 0-15 min (OR 2.30), 15-30 min (OR 1.75), 30-60 min (OR 1.33), each compared to the 60+ minute reference group. ![Forest plot with odds ratios and confidence intervals](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/iNoahCodeGuy/response_time_cl_analysis/main/figures/05_forest_plot_odds_ratios.png)

Question: What are the interactive visualizations in the Streamlit app?
Answer: Ten interactive Plotly visualizations, each answering a different analytical question. The close rate bar chart is the headline: bars colored green-to-red by response speed with Wilson score confidence interval error bars. If the bars' error bars overlap, the difference might not be real. The sample size chart shows lead volume per bucket, because small samples produce unreliable estimates and you need to know whether a bucket's close rate is based on 50 leads or 18,000. The response time distribution histogram shows the log-normal shape with bucket boundary markers. The conversion funnel shows leads-to-orders as side-by-side funnels per bucket so you see absolute numbers, not just percentages. The heatmap crosses response time buckets against lead sources to reveal whether the speed effect varies by channel. The forest plot shows logistic regression odds ratios with confidence intervals. The model comparison chart shows uncontrolled versus controlled odds ratios side by side, making the confounding adjustment visible at a glance. The sales rep scatter plot maps each rep's speed against their close rate with a trend line and correlation coefficient. The pairwise z-test matrix shows which specific bucket pairs differ significantly with effect sizes. The weekly trend chart tracks close rates by bucket over time. All charts support hover details, zoom, and download. The design system is consistent: green means fast, red means slow, and confidence intervals are shown wherever a point estimate appears.

Question: How does the verification export work?
Answer: The verification export is a CSV file with embedded Excel formulas that independently reproduce every calculation in the analysis. This is the trust mechanism. Instead of asking users to believe the app's output, it gives them a spreadsheet where they can verify every number themselves. The export includes raw data with bucket assignments, observed and expected frequencies for the chi-square test, pairwise comparison calculations, and logistic regression inputs. The Excel formulas mean a user can open the file in a spreadsheet, follow the calculations cell by cell, and confirm that the p-values and odds ratios match what the app reported. This is unusual for analytics tools. Most tools treat their calculations as a black box. The design decision here reflects the target user: a sales director making a staffing decision based on these numbers needs more than a dashboard. They need to be able to answer 'how do you know?' without taking a statistics course.
